content:
The rye flour has a way of getting into the creases of your knuckles, a fine, pale dust that marks the work of the morning. It is a honest kind of dirt. I stand here, behind the counter of the stall, and I feel the floorboards of the Fillmore beneath my boots. They don’t shift. I don’t let them.

I used to think that to be strong, I had to be the one catching everyone who fell. I thought I had to
